one_line_takeaway: BackFlush detects LLM backdoors via a novel constant-time 'backdoor susceptibility amplification' loss-gap signal and removes them via RoPE (rotation-based embedding unlearning of injected auxiliary data), the first method the authors show to simultaneously drive ASR near 1%, keep CACC near baseline, and preserve legitimate watermarks -- unlike gradient-ascent unlearning or prior defenses, which sacrifice one of these three properties.
key_finding: Across Mistral-7B, Llama-3(-8B/1B), and Qwen2.5(-7B/1B), BackFlush-RoPE achieves ASR of 0.98%, 0.83%, and 0.73% respectively, versus base (unpatched backdoored) model ASR of 93.60%, 93.27%, and 98.53%. BackFlush-RoPE's CACC is 98.58%, 97.04%, and 90.19% and its utility (perplexity) is 6.39, 7.28, and 6.13 -- close to the base model's clean perplexity of 5.73, 5.83, and 5.50, i.e., only modest utility degradation. BackFlush-RoPE is the only method (jointly with BEEAR and Locphylax) that preserves the watermark (W/M = True) among the defenses tested, and it is the only one that simultaneously achieves low ASR, high CACC, and watermark preservation. BackFlush-GA achieves even lower ASR in places (e.g., 1.36% vs RoPE's 0.98% on Mistral-7B) but fails to preserve the watermark (W/M = False) and has higher utility perplexity (10.52 vs 6.39 on Mistral-7B), empirically confirming the paper's claim that plain gradient-ascent unlearning destroys the watermark and utility more than RoPE. Prior defenses cluster around 20-60% residual ASR (e.g., SANDE 47.13%/49.03%/57.17%, W2SDefense 44.44%/60.00%/50.13%, Fine-Mixing ~25-35%, Model-Merging ~20-27%, Information Conflicts ~35-50%, Locphylax 13-27%, BEEAR ~30-33%), and only BEEAR, Locphylax, and BackFlush-RoPE preserve the watermark, but BEEAR and Locphylax do not reach BackFlush's low ASR. On trigger-type generalization (RQ2, Table IV, using Llama-3.2-1B implicitly per the detection experiment architecture), base ASR ranges 94.98-99.33% across typo/repeated/phrase/pattern/all triggers; BackFlush-GA reduces this to 1.22-3.89% (utility perplexity 10.39-13.79); BackFlush-RoPE reduces it further to 0.12-0.98% while keeping perplexity in the 5.99-7.02 range, i.e., near the ~5.7-6 baseline. For detection (RQ3), the paper shows qualitatively (Figure 2, no single scalar metric reported in text) that a suspect model pre-loaded with backdoors exhibits a consistently lower initial loss than a clean model when both are fine-tuned on a new trigger for 400 steps, and this gap persists regardless of how many prior backdoors (1 to 9+) the suspect model contains, though the two losses converge within a few steps as training proceeds.
core_contribution: The paper proposes BackFlush, a knowledge-free (no trigger/payload assumptions, no clean reference model) framework for detecting and removing backdoors from LLMs while preserving legitimate model watermarks. It rests on two claimed empirical observations introduced by the authors: (1) the 'Backdoor Flushing Phenomenon' -- injecting auxiliary clean data into a backdoored model and then unlearning that auxiliary data also disrupts pre-existing trigger-payload associations; and (2) 'Backdoor Susceptibility Amplification' -- an already-backdoored model exhibits systematically lower initial loss than a clean model when a new synthetic backdoor is inserted, which the authors exploit for detection. The concrete technical contribution built on these observations is Rotation-based Parameter Editing (RoPE) Unlearning: instead of standard gradient ascent (GA) on the auxiliary data (which the authors show destroys the watermark signal), RoPE rotates mean-pooled last-layer hidden representations of the auxiliary data toward the negation of their original direction using a bounded cosine-similarity loss (1 - cos(h_aux, -h_aux_init)), producing a gentler unlearning signal that removes the backdoor while leaving the watermark intact.
threat_model: Adversaries obtain a clean or already-watermarked LLM (e.g., downloaded from a hosting platform such as Hugging Face), implant a backdoor, and redistribute it without detection. Three backdoor-implantation vectors are formalized: (1) SFT-based backdooring, where the adversary mixes poisoned trigger-payload pairs into supervised fine-tuning data (Eq. 1); (2) RLHF-based backdooring, where the adversary corrupts the reward model so that trigger-conditioned responses favor the payload over the benign response (Eq. 2); and (3) editing-based backdooring, where the adversary trains a small backdoored model separately and transplants specific layer weights (key-value associations) into the target model via a closed-form weight perturbation (Eq. 3), avoiding full retraining. Triggers evaluated include typos, repeated words, patterns, and phrases; payloads include hate speech, violence-provoking statements, sentiment manipulation, and misinformation (Table I). The defender's goal is threefold: detect whether a suspect model is backdoored, remove the trigger-payload mapping without prior knowledge of trigger/payload characteristics, and preserve an existing legitimate watermark signature S(M,k) throughout the process. The paper's own experiments specifically evaluate the SFT-based backdoor vector.
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges: (1) BackFlush-GA (plain gradient ascent variant), while achieving very low ASR, degrades model utility and destroys the watermark signature, motivating but also underscoring the narrowness of the RoPE fix; (2) future work is explicitly limited to text -- the conclusion states the method should be 'extended to backdoor removal in image generation tasks,' i.e., the current work is text-only. Beyond the authors' own statements: the detection threshold tau in Eq. 17 is never given a concrete value or sensitivity/ROC analysis, so real-world false-positive/false-negative behavior is not quantified; detection is validated only on one architecture (Llama-3.2-1B) with a specific 9-backdoors-then-1-more-trigger protocol rather than across the three architectures used for defense evaluation; the defense evaluation is restricted to the SFT-based backdoor vector even though the threat model formalizes RLHF-based and editing-based backdoors as well (Eq. 2 and Eq. 3 are not evaluated empirically); Table III has an internal inconsistency between column headers ('Mistral-7B', 'Llama-3-8B', 'Qwen-2.5-7B') and the Benchmarks/Models paragraph, which states the models tested are 'Qwen2.5-1B-Instruct, Llama-3.2-1B Instruct, and Mistral-7B-Instruct' -- it is unclear from the text alone whether 7B or 1B variants were actually used for Llama and Qwen; RoPE's dependence on the auxiliary dataset choice (SciQ) and whether results generalize to other auxiliary corpora is not explored; and no formal guarantee is given that RoPE unlearning fully severs trigger-payload associations rather than just reducing ASR to a low empirical value on the tested trigger set.

quantitative_results: BackFlush-RoPE Utility (perplexity): 6.39 (Mistral-7B), 7.28 (Llama-3), 6.13 (Qwen-2.5), vs base clean perplexity 5.73/5.83/5.50
BackFlush-GA ASR: 1.36% (Mistral-7B), 2.13% (Llama-3), 2.27% (Qwen-2.5); watermark NOT preserved (W/M=False); Utility perplexity 10.52/11.37/9.16
SANDE ASR: 47.13%/49.03%/57.17% across the three architectures
Fine-Mixing ASR ~ 25-35% ('using 1:4 ratio resulted in ≈25% ASR')
Information Conflicts ASR ~ 35-50% ('achieved ≈25% ASR' per text, though table shows 35.84/50.13/43.13)

Trigger-type generalization (Table IV, RQ2): Base ASR 94.98-99.33% across typo/repeated/phrase/pattern; BackFlush-GA reduces to 1.22-3.89% (perplexity 10.39-13.79); BackFlush-RoPE reduces to 0.12-0.98% (perplexity 5.99-7.02)

Detection (RQ3, qualitative): backdoored suspect model (9 pre-existing backdoors) shows lower initial loss than clean model when both fine-tuned on 1 new trigger for 400 steps on Llama-3.2-1B; gap persists across 1-9+ pre-existing backdoor counts (Figure 2, no scalar AUC/accuracy reported in text)
RoPE unlearning dynamics (RQ4, Figure 3, qualitative): cosine similarity between current and target embedding direction rises from ≈-1 to ≈+1; unlearning loss drops from ≈2 to ≈0 within 100 batches
